title: Wet felted egg cosies
subtitle: Small merino cups that keep boiled eggs warm at the table
excerpt: Wet felt a set of four egg cosies from merino roving over a small balloon resist. Each cosy takes about 20 minutes of active work and fits a standard large hen's egg. The finished pieces are warm, washable, and durable enough for daily use.
sourceNotes: Small resist-felted vessels are a standard entry point for beginners in guild and community workshops. Method draws on the same balloon-resist principle documented in general wet-felting practice.

attrs.body: If using felting needles to tidy the rim, stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Always work over a foam pad.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: merino roving around a small water-filled balloon. The balloon inflated to roughly the size of a large egg gives the form. Four cosies use about 30 g of roving in total. Use a solid colour or blend two colours for a tweedy effect.

items.name: Felting net or old sheer fabric

content.text: Inflate four small balloons to roughly the size of a large hen's egg (about 6 x 4 cm). Tie the knots firmly. The balloon must be taut or the felt will be too loose to hold its shape when the balloon is removed.
Step 2: Wrap roving around the balloon

Pull roving into strips about 2 cm wide. Wrap the strips around the balloon in overlapping layers, covering the top and sides but leaving a 2 cm opening around the knot end. This open end becomes the cosy mouth. Apply two layers in opposite directions (horizontal, then vertical) so the fibres cross-hatch. Each layer should be even and without large gaps.

Place the wrapped balloon on the bamboo mat, cover with the felting net, and pour hot soapy water over it. Press through the net with flat palms until the roving is saturated. Begin rubbing in gentle circular strokes through the net for 5 minutes. Remove the net and rub the surface directly with soapy hands for another 10 minutes, turning the balloon and working all over the surface. The roving should begin to shrink and firm.
Step 4: Full and remove the balloon

Once the felt holds together without the net (it passes the pinch test: fibres resist separation), rinse in cold water to shock the fibres and tighten the felt. Let out the balloon by untying or piercing it. Work your hand into the cosy through the open mouth and stretch the rim slightly while still damp. Allow to dry over an upturned cup of the right size to hold the shape.

items.fix: Return to rubbing on the mat. The felt should pass the pinch test firmly before the balloon comes out.
items.cause: Insufficiently felted before removing the resist
items.problem: The cosy collapses flat when the balloon is removed

items.fix: Trim the mouth with scissors while still slightly damp and tuck any loose fibres inward, or needle-felt the rim on a foam pad to tighten the edge.
items.cause: The wrapping didn't leave a clean consistent opening around the balloon knot
items.problem: The mouth opening is ragged and uneven

items.fix: In the next piece, apply a third layer to any areas that look sparse before wetting out.
items.cause: The roving layers weren't evenly distributed
items.problem: Thin patches on the side of the cosy
